# css/

The toolkit's site stylesheet, plus the sources it's built from.

## What consumers reference

[`site.css`](site.css) — the composed file. Sites point at this from
their `shared-theme.yml`:

```yaml
site:
  options:
    style: _toolkit/css/site.css
```

**Do not edit `site.css` directly.** It's generated by
[`../bin/build-css.sh`](../bin/build-css.sh) and gets overwritten on
the next build.

## Sources

| File | What it contains |
|---|---|
| [`sources/_imports.css`](sources/_imports.css) | `@import` statements (Google Fonts, etc.). Bubbled to the top of the composed `site.css`. |
| [`sources/_layout.css`](sources/_layout.css) | Hero block kinds (`.split-image`, `.justified`), the `{button}` role, footer grid. Org-neutral. |
| [`../plugins/myst-mermaid/css/mermaid.css`](../plugins/myst-mermaid/css/mermaid.css) | Mermaid plugin CSS (dual-render toggling, theme palette, font override). Composed in only if the plugin is bundled. |

## How composition works

[`bin/build-css.sh`](../bin/build-css.sh) does roughly:

```bash
# 1. Collect every `@import` line from every source — bubble to top.
# 2. Banner.
# 3. Concatenate all non-@import lines in fixed order:
#       sources/_layout.css
#       plugins/myst-mermaid/css/mermaid.css
# 4. Write to css/site.css.
```

So @imports come first (CSS spec requirement), followed by every rule
from every source. The script normalizes blank lines and adds a clear
"GENERATED — do not edit" banner.

## Why composition rather than runtime `@import`?

MyST's `site.options.style` only accepts a single CSS path. Cross-file
`@import` directives are passed through to the browser, but MyST
doesn't serve the imported sibling files — they 404 at runtime.

Composition at build time gives us:
- Modular sources (edit one concern at a time).
- A single CSS file for consumers (one `style:` entry, no surprises).
- @imports in the right place (top of the file, as the spec requires).

## Running the build

```bash
./bin/build-css.sh
```

The script is idempotent. Re-run any time a source changes. Commit
both the source file edits **and** the regenerated `site.css`.

A pre-commit hook checks that the regenerated `site.css` matches the
committed one — drift fails CI.
